title: How to Handle "I Need to Think About It" (Word for Word)
dek: It's the sentence that ends more deals than any market condition: "I need to think about it." Here's the truth about it, and the exact words that turn it around.
heroAlt: How to Handle "I Need to Think About It" (Word for Word)

blocks.html: It's rarely about thinking. "I need to think about it" is a fog that means "you haven't answered something." A concern was born earlier in the process, went unspoken, and grew in silence. By decision time it comes out wearing armor.

blocks.text: An objection is a question wearing armor.
Never fight it. Agree, then open it:

blocks.html: "Totally fair, this is where you're going to live, you should be sure. Can I ask, when you say think it over, is it the apartment itself, the number, or the timing? Because those are three different conversations and I can actually help with all three."

Delivered calmly, this converts fog into fact. Nine times out of ten they tell you the real thing: "honestly it's about $200 over budget," or "my partner hasn't seen it," or "my start date might move." Every one of those is a solvable problem. A fog is not.

blocks.text: If they genuinely want to sleep on it

blocks.html: Some people do. Respect it and stay the professional: "Great. Units like this do move, so here's what I'll do: I'll check status first thing tomorrow and text you either way, so you're deciding with real information." No pressure applied, urgency stays honest, door stays open, and you've scheduled the next contact.

The best agents hear this objection least, because they answer it before it forms: budget clarity before the tour, partner invited to the showing, timing discussed up front. Remove the no before it arrives.
Every objection script in sequence is in The Luxury Leasing Playbook, twenty dollars. Better agents win.
